transcript:
Proverbs chapter 18, verse 24: "There are friends who pretend to be friends, but there is a friend who sticks closer than a brother." It's true. Every one of us — I don't know how old you were the first time you recognized that there are people who are friends for a season, friends who are only fair-weather friends, and the moment trouble comes, they're on their way out the door. However old you were when you discovered that, it's one of those lessons that is almost invaluable. Because imagine going through life not knowing that. It's a painful lesson, but at the same time, when you flip it around, you realize what an incredible gift it is when you do find a true friend. A faithful friend is a sturdy shelter, as Proverbs tells us. And a true friend comes along so infrequently that when you find one, the only response is thanksgiving.

So speaking of gifts, here is Matthew chapters 5, 6, and 7 — the Sermon on the Mount. It starts with the Beatitudes, and here is Jesus going up the mountain to give a new law, the fulfillment of the law. That's important for a couple of reasons. In the Old Testament, Moses went up the mountain and came down with the law, the commandments of God. So here is Jesus as the new Moses, who's going to give not only a new law but also bring people to a new Exodus, a new place of freedom.

Don't miss that parallel: Jesus goes up the mountain and gives the fulfillment of the law, because he makes a very important point: "I did not come to abolish the law or the prophets. I came to fulfill them." That's one of the reasons why, as Christians, we recognize that this was not meant to be a break between Judaism and Christianity, but rather the fulfillment of Judaism. Our roots — the reason we've spent 200-plus days reading the Old Testament — is because that's our heritage. We come from the Jewish people and God's covenant of love with them. Christianity doesn't pop out of nowhere. It is the fulfillment of everything that every faithful Jewish person longed for.

There are so many things we could point out about the Sermon on the Mount. In chapter five, Jesus gives new laws: "You have heard it was said, but I say to you" — about anger, adultery, divorce, swearing oaths, retaliation, love for enemies. One of the things underneath all of them is that Jesus is not only saying those things are bad. He's also saying, "I want to diagnose your heart." The problem isn't just that you kill people — the problem is that it's an option for you. The problem isn't just that you commit adultery — it's that you want to.

Jesus is getting past the action and getting to the heart. The action is still prohibited, but he's elevating this to reveal that the issue is not the sin itself — it's the broken heart that commits the sin. And the beautiful thing is that Jesus isn't just diagnosing the problem; he's also offering the remedy. He himself is the remedy. He's saying, "Let me be the one who enters into that broken heart. Let me be the one who changes your heart" — so that we're not conformed to the world around us where it's normal to hate those who hate you, but conformed to his heart, where we love those who hate us and we pray for those who persecute us and we give when people want to take from us.

One of the remarkable things about chapter six is that Jesus talks about almsgiving, prayer, and fasting. He says there are some people who give alms and pray and fast so that others may see them — and that's the wrong motivation. The opinion that should matter most is the Father's. Notice that Jesus says "when you give alms," "when you fast," "when you pray" — not "if." He's assuming we are doing these things. As disciples of Jesus Christ, if we are not regularly giving alms, praying, and fasting, then we're not doing what he's told us to do. But when we do it, the question is: who are we doing it for?

Jesus is also reminding us to remember who we're talking to when we pray. He says, "When you pray, do not babble like the pagans, who think that because of their many words they will be heard." They thought saying the right incantation could get the attention of a god. But Jesus says, "You have a Father in heaven." If you just call upon his name, he's already attentive to you. You don't have to fight for his attention — you already have it. In fact, the Father is constantly fighting for our attention. When we give it to him in prayer, it's an act of love.

Lastly, Jesus says, "Not everyone who says to me, 'Lord, Lord,' shall enter the kingdom of heaven, but only the one who does the will of my Father in heaven." Those who say, "We prophesied in your name, cast out demons in your name, did many mighty works in your name," he says, "Depart from me." Why? Because you might have done incredible things, but did you do the Father's will?

This is the secret to holiness: doing what the Father has asked. If I'm doing my own will all the time, Jesus could say, "I don't even know who you are, because you don't know who I am." But if we say, "God, with my weak heart, my broken heart, with your grace, I strive to simply do the Father's will at all times" — that's what holiness is. That's what it is to know Jesus and to be known by Jesus. Not simply to say "Lord, Lord," but to do the will of our heavenly Father. Faith has to be put into action, or it is worthless.

discussion.text: there is a friend who sticks closer than a brother
I did not come to abolish the law or the prophets. I came to fulfill them
we love those who hate us and we pray for those who persecute us
there are some people who give alms and pray and fast so that others may see them
do not babble like the pagans, who think that because of their many words they will be heard
Not everyone who says to me, 'Lord, Lord,' shall enter the kingdom of heaven, but only the one who does the will of my Father in heaven
